text: Doctors in Training - Meeting Notes from 10 January 2017 Issues 1. Culture of the hospital/ED – there are concerns that the message being received by junior doctors is all about length of stay/WEAT/NEAT/LOS/proposed D/C date, and not about patient care. They feel that if the focus is on patient care and not the statistics, the rest will follow. a. Examples given where demands in ED to put in bed slips has preceded adequate medical review. b. Inadequate time to provide good discharge advice to patients. 2. “Admin Churn” where junior doctors are swamped with inefficient processes/IT systems that increase workload and decrease efficiency; a. Examples given of the difficulties with NaCS and the potential to link with BossNet which would improve efficiency in doing discharge summaries, but this is not seen as a priority. b. Another example is booking patients in for a procedure requires 3 different IT programmes and multiple phone calls. They also raised that to complete the discharge summaries/scripts, they had to leave the ward rounds and miss out on valuable bedside teaching. c. Excessive number of phone calls to some positions. 3. The message being received about BOT was interpreted as an effort to promote early discharges and pushing patients to the wards. a. It was seen by some JMO’s as them “not doing their job properly”, being asked constantly by the volunteers about completing discharge summaries/scripts rather than about patient care. They felt that the wrong question was being asked. They would’ve preferred “What can we do to support you in doing your job better?” rather than “when will the discharge/script be ready?” or “When will the patient be ready to go home?” b. In addition, many felt that it was about improving statistics, but questioned whether patient outcomes were indeed better. They are waiting on the data around the number of MET calls, mortality stats and 28 day readmission. 4. Major issues with ED culture were raised. Many examples given about: a. Inappropriate admissions – where if patients were worked up properly then they wouldn’t need to even be admitted. b. Inappropriate team to be admitted under – where patients are admitted under the wrong team, and then an inefficient system of trying to transfer the patient to the correct team, whereas if the patients were more thoroughly worked up in ED, then the correct team would be notified initially by ED. c. Inappropriate transfer of unstable patients to the ward – no senior review prior to d/c from ED, no senior plan in notes, ADDS score >4. 5. Engagement of DiTs in BoT: a. The current engagement strategy through line management was ineffective. DiTs were keen to be involved in the planning of the next BoT exercise. Possible solutions 1. Improve the IT systems to make DiT work more efficient – eg. To improve the ease of using NACS and link it with BossNet. 2. Ensure ED are following hospital process – i.e. ensuring that ED patients being admitted to FSH are being admitted to the appropriate team, are safe for transfer and the admitting team is informed. Ideally clerking/med charts should be completed in ED a. Safety checklist pre patient leaving ED. b. Track the numbers of inappropriate referrals, poor transfers and poor outcomes. c. Another system for escalating issues (other than CIMS forms which they don’t have time to complete) needs to be introduced to capture some of this data. Who do they speak to and what happens? 3. Review ED culture and way of communicating – too adversarial - particularly around bed slips etc. 4. Improvements in communication with JMO’s: a. Ensure the focus is on patient care (not KPI’s). b. Empower them to drive local solutions within their teams. c. Regular meetings with Heads of Specialty during the term about issues and possible solutions. d. Need to feel that the hospital is working towards reducing their administrative burden so they can practice being doctors. e. Suggestions for a monthly newsletter featuring a section “Ask the Executive”, where questions can be asked by all staff, and answered. f. Communication via medical education is one effective way to communicate with JMO’s. 5. Need for adequate feedback of actions to be taken out of these meetings with JMO’s (to reflect they’ve been heard and have power to enact positive change). 6. Increase number of junior staff for the workload and allow leave – note additional RMOs have been recruited to facilitate leave cover in the early months of the year. 7. Additional ED staff such as a triage doctor and an extra admitting doctor in ED (to remove pressure from ward registrars to come and admit patients). 8. Mobile phones (rather than the current pagers) would be better for the busy registrar positions that spend much of the day answering the phone – where the ability to receive and send texts to solve issues would be helpful and an efficient way to communicate. 9. Nursing checklist for transit lounge so patients are not transferred inappropriately there. 10. With regards to BOT specifically: a. Include DiTs in BoT working group. b. Keep same staff in the same units for next BOT so they don’t keep asking the same questions, but get to know a unit well and help drive local solutions. c. Better training for volunteers in the right questions to be asking. d. Make it really easy for DiTs to report safety breaches. e. Ask WLOs to shadow a DiT one day during BoT. 11. To improve understanding of the DiTs workload: a. Follow around a specific DiT for a day (eg. like “Undercover Boss”) to really understand the issues on the ground. b. More frequent open meetings. c. Small focus group to brainstorm ways to better manage predicted acute demand within the available beds and considering elective, educational and work-life balance perspectives.
